steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "scientists [blank] for evidence of self-awareness in snakes...adapted the test to foreground smell." The word "adapted" is already the sentence's main verb. The blank cannot take another finite verb without creating a run-on or requiring a conjunction; it needs a participle that modifies "scientists" as a participial phrase.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'searched': A finite past-tense verb. Placing two finite verbs in a row ("searched...adapted") creates a run-on — there is no conjunction or punctuation joining them as independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice B — 'were searching': A finite past-progressive verb. Same problem: two main verbs with no coordinating conjunction. ✗
  • Choice C — 'have searched': A finite present-perfect verb. Same problem; also shifts tense inconsistently with the past-tense "adapted." ✗
  • Choice D — 'searching': A present participle. It creates the modifying phrase "scientists searching for evidence..." with "adapted" as the sole main verb, giving the sentence a single clear predicate. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
